g.data-narration: Welcome to Forests in Data Structures - an essential concept extending tree structures.
A forest is a collection of disjoint trees - independent hierarchical structures with no shared vertices.
g.text: • Each tree has its own root

g.data-narration: A tree has exactly one root and connected components, while a forest has multiple roots and disconnected trees.
Formally, a forest is an acyclic graph where all connected components are trees - disjoint independent structures.
g.text: A forest is a set of zero or more disjoint trees.
• n trees (n>1) → n roots, no inter-tree edges

g.data-narration: Parent pointer representation stores each node with a reference to its parent - roots have NULL parent pointers.
g.text: Each node stores a pointer to its parent. Roots have NULL (-1) parent.

g.data-narration: Child-sibling representation uses first-child and next-sibling pointers to convert general trees to binary tree format.
g.text: Each node has FirstChild and NextSibling pointers.

g.data-narration: The left-child-right-sibling transformation converts any general tree to binary tree form for algorithm reuse.
g.text: Transform general trees to binary trees: First Child → Left Child, Next Sibling → Right Child

g.data-narration: Let's convert a general tree with multiple children to its binary tree representation step by step.
Forest operations include finding roots, adding trees, joining forests, splitting forests, and merging trees.
